content.problemStatement:
An array AAA of size NNN is called good if the following conditions hold:


For every pair (l,r)(l, r)(l,r) (1≤l≤r≤N)(1 \le l \le r \le N)(1≤l≤r≤N), Al⊕Al+1⊕...⊕Ar≠0A_l \oplus A_{l+1} \oplus ... \oplus A_{r} \ne 0Al​⊕Al+1​⊕...⊕Ar​=0. (where ⊕\oplus⊕ denotes the bitwise XOR operation.)
JJ has an array AAA of size NNN. He wants to convert the array to a good array. To do so he can perform the following operation multiple times:


Pick an index iii such that (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) and set Ai:=XA_i := XAi​:=X where 0≤X<1010100 \le X \lt 10^{10^{10}}0≤X<101010.
Find the **minimum** number of operations required to convert AAA into a good array.


Input Format

The first line contains TTT - the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN - the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the **minimum** number of operations required to convert the array AAA into a good array.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Then T test cases follow. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum number of operations required to make the array good.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: while scanning, a duplicate prefix xor appears twice, so two operations are needed. Test case 2: every element creates a duplicate, requiring three changes. Test case 3: all prefix xors stay distinct, so no change is required.
Prefixes: 0,5,4,6,1 – all distinct, array is already good.
Duplicates appear after the second and fourth elements, leading to two necessary operations.

solution.approach:
A subarray l…r has xor zero iff the prefix xor before l (Pl‑1) equals the prefix xor at r (Pr). Therefore the whole array is good exactly when all prefix xors (including P0 = 0) are pairwise distinct. While scanning the array we keep a set of prefix xors that have appeared in the current segment. If the new prefix xor already exists in the set, we must perform an operation (change the current element to some value that makes the prefix xor new and distinct). After such an operation we start a new segment: the set is cleared and we insert the previous prefix xor and the current one (they are now the only two values in the new segment). The number of times we have to start a new segment equals the minimum number of required changes. This greedy strategy is optimal because any duplicate prefix xor forces at least one change inside the segment, and performing the change exactly at the current position never harms future decisions.

Algorithm per test case:
1. ans = 0, cur = 0.
2. Create an empty hash set S and insert 0 (the initial prefix xor).
3. For each element a in the array:
   a. cur ^= a.
   b. If cur is already in S:
        ans++;
        clear S;
        insert previous prefix xor (cur ^ a) and cur into S.
      else insert cur into S.
4. Output ans.

The algorithm runs in O(N) time per test case and uses O(N) total memory for the hash set (the set is cleared frequently, so the peak size is at most the length of the current segment).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(ΣN) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) in the worst case for the hash set.
